learning_objectives: Identify unique challenges of managing projects across national boundaries
Analyze political, economic, social, and technological factors in international projects
Apply PESTLE analysis to an international project context

lecture_notes.introduction: International projects operate across political, legal, cultural, and economic boundaries that domestic projects do not face.
key_concepts: PESTLE analysis: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental factors
Country risk: political stability, regulatory environment, corruption, infrastructure
Currency and exchange rate risks
Legal framework differences: contract enforcement, labor laws, intellectual property
Time zone challenges: coordination across 8+ hours of difference
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a PESTLE analysis for a construction project in Southeast Asia

review.value: Systematic environmental scan ensuring all macro-environmental factors are considered for international projects
Political instability, weak legal systems, and corruption can derail technically sound projects

learning_objectives: Apply Hofstede's and Meyer's cultural dimension frameworks
Adapt communication style for different cultural contexts
Build trust in multicultural project teams

lecture_notes.introduction: Cultural differences are not obstacles to overcome but realities to navigate. Effective international PMs are culturally fluent.
key_concepts: Hofstede's dimensions: power distance, individualism, masculinity, uncertainty avoidance, long-term orientation
Meyer's culture map: communicating, evaluating, leading, deciding, trusting, disagreeing, scheduling, persuading
High-context vs. low-context communication
Task-based vs. relationship-based trust
Cultural adaptation: adjusting your approach without losing authenticity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing communication approaches for a project meeting with Japanese, German, and Brazilian team members
setup_requirements: Meyer's Culture Map profiles for selected countries

review.value: In task-based cultures (US, Germany), trust comes from reliability; in relationship-based cultures (China, Brazil), trust comes from personal connection
Some cultures confront directly (France, Israel); others avoid direct confrontation (Japan, Thailand); the PM must read the room

learning_objectives: Navigate different legal systems affecting international projects
Manage regulatory compliance across multiple jurisdictions
Structure international contracts for risk protection
readings: ICC Guide to International Business Law (selected chapters)

lecture_notes.introduction: International projects must comply with multiple legal systems simultaneously. Ignorance of local law is not a defense.
key_concepts: Legal systems: common law (US, UK), civil law (Europe, Latin America), religious law, customary law
Jurisdiction and governing law clauses in contracts
International dispute resolution: ICC arbitration, UNCITRAL rules
Regulatory compliance: environmental, labor, data privacy (GDPR), anti-corruption (FCPA, UK Bribery Act)
Import/export controls and sanctions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reviewing an international construction contract and identifying jurisdiction, dispute resolution, and compliance clauses

review.value: The contract must specify which country's law governs; this affects interpretation of every clause
US Foreign Corrupt Practices Act prohibits bribing foreign officials; applies to US companies and their agents worldwide

class_schedule.topic: International Project Logistics and Supply Chain
learning_objectives: Plan logistics for international project materials and equipment

lecture_notes.introduction: Getting materials, equipment, and people to where they need to be across international boundaries is a major challenge in international projects.
key_concepts: Incoterms: EXW, FOB, CIF, DDP and their risk/cost transfer points
International shipping: ocean freight, air freight, intermodal, customs clearance
Supply chain risk: single-source dependency, geopolitical disruption, natural disasters
Local content requirements: many countries require use of local materials or labor
Logistics planning: lead times, customs delays, storage, last-mile delivery
lecture_notes.demonstration: Planning the logistics for delivering industrial equipment from Germany to a construction site in Indonesia

review.value: Define exactly when risk and cost transfer from seller to buyer; DDP means seller bears all risk to delivery; EXW means buyer bears all risk from pickup
Many countries require a percentage of materials, labor, or services to be sourced locally; non-compliance can result in project shutdown

class_schedule.topic: International Project Case Studies and Best Practices
learning_objectives: Analyze success factors and failure patterns in international projects
Apply lessons learned from major international project case studies
Develop a personal framework for international project management

lecture_notes.introduction: Learning from the successes and failures of major international projects provides practical wisdom that textbooks cannot.
key_concepts: Success factors: strong local partnerships, cultural adaptability, regulatory preparation, risk reserves
Failure patterns: cultural arrogance, underestimating regulatory complexity, poor stakeholder engagement
Megaproject challenges: optimism bias, strategic misrepresentation, lock-in effects
International PM competencies: cultural intelligence, adaptability, resilience, political savvy
Building international PM capability: expatriate assignments, multicultural team experience, language skills
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the Panama Canal expansion project as an international PM case study

review.value: International megaprojects consistently underestimate costs by 50-100% and schedules by 25-50%
Successful international projects invest heavily in local partnerships for cultural, regulatory, and logistical knowledge

class_schedule.topic: International Project Risk and Currency Management
learning_objectives: Identify risks unique to international projects
Apply hedging strategies for currency risk
readings: PMI Practice Standard for Risk Management (international supplement)

lecture_notes.introduction: International projects face all domestic project risks plus a layer of country-specific, cultural, and geopolitical risks.
key_concepts: Currency risk: transaction exposure, translation exposure, economic exposure
Hedging instruments: forward contracts, options, natural hedging
Political risk: expropriation, sanctions, policy changes, civil unrest
Force majeure in international context: broader scope than domestic projects
International risk register: standard risk categories plus country-specific categories
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating currency exposure for a project with costs in three currencies and designing a hedging strategy

review.value: Locks in an exchange rate for a future date; eliminates currency uncertainty but also eliminates upside
Matching revenues and costs in the same currency; reduces exposure without financial instruments

questions.question: You are managing a software project with teams in the US (low power distance, low context) and Japan (high power distance, high context). During a video meeting, the Japanese team lead says 'That approach is interesting' when reviewing your proposed architecture. What does this likely mean, and how should you respond?
questions.answer: In high-context Japanese communication, 'that is interesting' is typically an indirect way of expressing disagreement or concern rather than genuine interest. Direct criticism, especially of a senior person's proposal, would be considered disrespectful in a high power distance culture. The PM should: (1) Not take the statement at face value, (2) Follow up privately with the Japanese team lead one-on-one where they may feel more comfortable expressing concerns, (3) Ask open-ended questions like 'What aspects would you improve?' rather than yes/no questions, (4) Create a written feedback mechanism (anonymous if needed) to capture honest input from high-context team members.

rubric.key: Identifies cultural factors (high context, power distance)

questions.question: Explain the difference between task-based trust and relationship-based trust, and describe how this affects how you should start a project with a new team member from Brazil vs. from the Netherlands.
questions.answer: Task-based trust (common in Netherlands, US, Denmark) is built through demonstrated competence and reliability in completing work. You earn trust by delivering results. Relationship-based trust (common in Brazil, China, India) is built through personal connection, shared meals, and getting to know each other as people. You earn trust by investing in the relationship. With a new Dutch team member: start with the project work, share your credentials and track record, deliver on commitments early, and trust will develop naturally. With a new Brazilian team member: invest time in personal conversation first, learn about their family and interests, have a social dinner before diving into project work, and be patient because relationship building takes time but creates deep loyalty.

quizzes.topic: International Legal, Logistics, and Risk Management

questions.question: Your project needs to ship $500,000 of equipment from the US to a construction site in Nigeria. The buyer proposes FOB terms and the seller proposes DDP terms. From the buyer's perspective, which is better and why?
questions.answer: From the buyer's perspective, DDP (Delivered Duty Paid) is better because the seller bears all costs and risks of delivering the goods to the final destination in Nigeria, including shipping, insurance, customs clearance, import duties, and local delivery. Under FOB (Free on Board), risk transfers to the buyer once the goods are loaded on the vessel at the US port, meaning the buyer bears all shipping, insurance, customs, and delivery risks and costs in Nigeria. For a buyer unfamiliar with Nigerian customs procedures, import regulations, and local logistics, DDP transfers those complexities to the seller who may have more experience. The trade-off is that the seller will price DDP higher to account for the additional risk.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies DDP as better for buyer
Accurately describes risk transfer under both terms

questions.question: A project has costs of EUR 2M and USD 3M, with revenues entirely in USD. The current EUR/USD rate is 1.10. Explain the currency risk and propose one hedging strategy.
questions.answer: The currency risk is that the EUR 2M cost could increase in USD terms if the euro strengthens against the dollar. At the current rate of 1.10, EUR 2M = USD 2.2M, making total costs USD 5.2M. If the euro strengthens to 1.20, EUR 2M = USD 2.4M, increasing total costs to USD 5.4M (a $200,000 overrun). Hedging strategy: purchase a forward contract to buy EUR 2M at a locked rate (e.g., 1.10 or near-current rate) for the dates when euro-denominated payments are due. This eliminates the uncertainty by fixing the exchange rate. The cost of the forward contract (a small premium) provides certainty that the EUR costs will not exceed the budgeted USD equivalent.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the currency risk exposure
Quantifies the potential impact with an example
Proposes a valid hedging strategy with explanation

major_assignments.title: International Project Management Plan
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive project management plan for an international project spanning three countries, addressing cultural, legal, logistical, and risk management challenges.
objectives: Conduct PESTLE analysis for each country involved
Design a cross-cultural communication and team management plan
Develop an international procurement and logistics plan
Create a comprehensive international risk register with response strategies
deliverables: PESTLE analysis for 3 countries (2 pages each)
Cross-cultural communication plan with cultural dimension analysis (3-4 pages)
International procurement and logistics plan including Incoterms selection (3 pages)
International risk register with 20+ risks and response strategies
Currency risk management plan with hedging approach (2 pages)

criteria: All six PESTLE dimensions covered for each country
Cultural dimensions correctly analyzed for each country
Communication strategies tailored to each culture
Team management approach addresses trust, feedback, and decision-making differences
Incoterms selected and justified for each procurement
Logistics timeline accounts for customs and transport
20+ risks identified across country and cultural categories
Currency risk quantified and hedging approach specified
Response strategies are specific and actionable
Thorough PESTLE analysis for all countries

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on cultural dimensions, legal frameworks, and international risk

midterm_examination.description: Cultural communication, PESTLE analysis, and legal frameworks

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all international PM topics

class_participation.description: Participation in cross-cultural simulations, case studies, and logistics exercises

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Cross-cultural simulation exercises require in-person participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Cross-cultural simulations are collaborative. The international PM plan assignment is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Country data must be sourced from credible publications and properly cited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Internet access for country research. Spreadsheet application for currency risk calculations. Presentation software for PESTLE analysis.
